speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] is tape five. It's the 23rd of July 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, what do you think would have happened if they hadn't found the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we could have been penalized some way. Or the anger of the guards would have hit us later on if they would have blamed the guards for it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, they did us a favor in letting us swimming on that hot day. That was the first and last day of swimming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually Friday nights we could have a bath, a shower, and disinfect our clothing, which was, after about an hour going back to the barracks, was again full of lice because they didn't disinfect the, the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every Friday you were allowed to bath.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or shower. What did they have for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Friday, or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, every Saturday afternoon. We worked till about midday Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saturday afternoon you could have a shower. You were taken to the shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you soap and towels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they had soap and towels. We-- normally, we had only cold water inside the camp. A long stretch of water pipes with steps every three feet away. You could wash. So it was really terrible. Sometimes it froze over and cracked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there only cold water there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only cold water. Outside once a week, we-- you had hot water. And these-- we were fed by [INAUDIBLE] organization. And we had thousands of us. We were 500 from [INAUDIBLE] and 500 from another area put into here. 1,000 Jews in that camp. We had much better provisions than the other 10,000 had who were next to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were supplied by the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were working for the [INAUDIBLE] in a different supply. Well, the only SS we had was a lieutenant who had a deputy. His name was [? Bush. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The lieutenant, lieutenant was [? Bush? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he, he was an SS lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was quite friendly with the prisoners. On rainy days he, he didn't even ask us to-- he was counting us inside the barracks. And the other, his deputy, was named [? Meyer. ?] He, he was-- what rank would that be? Not officer. The highest rank before officer. Sergeant Major. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something like that, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was very, very rough and shouting at every one of us. Now that, that [? Bush ?] really, he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think some, some people witnessed after liberation, put some good words in for him, whether it helped him, [INAUDIBLE], or not. But not for [? Meyer. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: John, what, what happened afterwards? How long did you-- first of all, how old were you now? It's coming to winter 1944. So you were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nearly 18.

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 In December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you, you-- how long did you have to still spend in Allach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in Allach just, just four days before liberation. Still working in Allach in different little places. The whole 1,000 men all under [INAUDIBLE] organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- so you came [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still working with my father in the same group. And just go back to where I was sidetracked. When, when I had so much raw potatoes, what to do with it? And the German engineer from [INAUDIBLE] asked me to get a shovel and build him a one-man bunker against air raids. Because night time he, he could jump out from his little room, from his hot bed a couple meters away, and into that hole. They were fox holes, sort of. So I started digging and it was still not too cold. No one frozen. There were a lot of roots there because it was bush setting. And I build a sink and the [INAUDIBLE] the engineers held me to. We put four, four, four by two or four by four timbers in the four corners and a corrugated roof with some soil on it. And he could have a vision, sloping slightly, and he had a vision all around open about two foot. But we made it deeper so we could hide the potatoes. And he, he was sitting on our potatoes on straw put over timber, like a little bridge. We filled it up with potatoes. So we, we, we had no potato problem, where to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, on an ongoing basis, every time you had potatoes--

speaker: interviewee
text: When, when we had more potatoes left over raw, we put it so it doesn't get frozen in winter. It had straw over it and timber. And when there was an alarm, the German was sit-- standing on top of it. And it was only a few meters from, from his building. And once he came back to his room. And I, I was-- I was having a two-liter dish, enamel dish, which I used sometimes on my head, too, on different bombings like a helmet. Well, I used the baked potatoes and I bought margarine and bloodwurst from, from the camp. And baked it again, which is already baked on top of his oven. And the smell hit him. And he wanted a little. He asked me a, a little bit of it. And in the same camp, once we-- the group was keen on, on meat. There was no meat. So they decided to kill a dog. They shoot, shoot a dog. The guards will shoot it. They were Italian guards and a German Sergeant commanding. The German Sergeant made a condition that he wanted the liver for himself. So they butchered the, the dog and cut it up. And boiled it on an open copper kettle in the open fire and had a meal. That, that-- they wanted a, a variety from, from the camp meal so they cooked the dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards-- the guards themselves had the, the, the liver. There was no, no wurst. They had-- didn't have it so good. Their food wasn't good either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in working here till when it started getting cold. And my father got-- thumb got infected from a cut. And it got worse. And the local [INAUDIBLE], that's the little ambulance place in one barrack, were doctors from our town, from where I studied. I knew some of them. Dr. [INAUDIBLE] and a few more. Jewish doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish doctors, yeah. And they told me, your father can get through the winter months in Dachau main hospital. We can arrange him to be sent there. And that hospital is under, occasionally under Swiss supervision. And it's good conditions in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked my father if he wants to go. Said he, he, he doesn't care if, if it's OK. So they sent him over there and I never heard of him. Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again. And what happened after liberation, a young officer, American [INAUDIBLE] came with me into the administration building in Dachau. And I found the exact date when he died. It was all registered in, in the hospital registry. He died on 28 January, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name? What was his first name, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon, Shlomo Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the hospital, he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hospital. The-- so he didn't die from that thing. At worst he could have lost the thumb. But Fleck typhus broke out in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid, which, which is a different typhoid from food. It comes out in marks on your skin and high temperature for two weeks. If you pass 10 days, you survive. You survive. You have after effects. Loss of memory. Loss of hair. Some people survived it and my father didn't. And all the crew and everybody caught it. Because it goes, that is spread with lice, not with food. If, if-- everywhere had lice in, in these camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. People coming from other camps into the hospital, they bring the lice. There were-- there were so much lice you cannot imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have lice, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I had. When, when, when you had a, a belt and the lice hard for them to get through the belt, you could just go around the belt and have nearly a handful of it. Yeah. See, in inside Allach when winter came, well, I didn't know my father died. He just went, went in beginning of December to, to that place. Started getting cold nights and started snowing. And we found two big heaps of potato, which was actually for us, put into straw for freeze-- not freezing, inside the camp territory. About three, four meters high, like a pyramid shape. And covered with earth. And they-- each time they had to use 200, 300 kilo, they opened it up and they pulled it out and they put it back when one gets frozen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 5 o'clock in the morning, I got up with a few more young chaps and we went and started pinching potatoes there. We opened up where it was half closed and soft. We, we put the potatoes on steel wire, one after the other, and put it through the chimney. Because the barrack had a-- the room where the capo lived, a small room where the head of the barrack lived. And he used to fire his little stove. So we let it in from the roof top. It was snow covered already, the roof. So you had to watch if, if the guards want-- sometimes they put projectors around, going in circles. When the projector wasn't on, climb up on the roof, let the potato singe, leave it in for about 30 minutes. They were burned, black hole like outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then we distributed. Everybody got two potatoes. And when they counted you in the freezing morning, kept your warm in your pockets, two hot potatoes. Plus you, you ate it, too. The middle didn't burn. Only the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did on the top of his chimney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now these, these Germans gave us German mark distributed for different groups in BMW and where we worked. We got here sometimes 50 marks, sometimes 200 mark for the 50. So the capo-- he was a Yugoslav man-- he said, we've got 100 mark. Two mark each for the group. But we didn't get the money. They had a canteen inside the camp which sold goods which you couldn't use. Sold you pen and pencil. You can't write a letter to anybody. You could do drawings with it. And very seldom, like for Christmas, they had some cheap lollies in there. So you can buy-- they were given one mark only to spend on lollies in there. You had a card in, in the canteen with the amount of money your group earned. And then they deducted whatever you bought there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had shoe cream. Well, you had wooden shoes. Who, who cared about cleaning shoes? Nobody bought it. So it was a-- was really a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Useless things.

speaker: interviewer
text: --really a joke.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it canteen. It was useless, completely useless. But there-- it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in, in the beginning we get 600 gram bread, which was quite a lot of bread for a prisoner, which went down slowly to four people a bread. Bread was total 800 gram. Then six and then eight in the end. There were shortages. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What can I also tell you from Allach? Were they trying to starve you? Or they were-- or they were-- didn't have themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they, they didn't. That was the ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't, didn't have more in the store, so you had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have enough of more for themselves either.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And on the retreat from France, we, we got pushed together more people. And we were leaving [INAUDIBLE] barracks empty there. We had to go out to pick up a group of prisoners from the railway station and bring them in on, on trailers, push rubber thing. They were in such a shape that you could lift-- we weren't too strong ourselves. But I could lift a man under my arm. He was only bone.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now, they couldn't stand on their feet. So we found among them one from our town, from Oradea. And the doctors there started giving him bit by bit a bit of sugar, a bit of biscuits. And the ambulance had a bit of biscuits until it took about two weeks to make him alive again. Some died after a day or two. They couldn't survive. You couldn't eat, couldn't do nothing. Was down to 30 kilo. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Sometimes we, we, we were-- we were ashamed when they saw us in conditions twice as good as they were in weight. Some could only look at you, couldn't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were from all over? They didn't-- were not necessarily from--

speaker: interviewee
text: That transport came from Kochendorf, which was in, in southern Germany and, and it was a salt mine. They were working in the salt mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, what did you do? How long did you still spend in Allach? You came there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to there, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: About September.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1944 and you stayed there until you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till, till the end of April. About 25th of April, roughly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there almost close on the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated 1st of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And taken out of Allach on a Saturday. We had to walk to the BMW's railway station which is called Karlsfeld. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now from the camp to Karlsfeld only Russians from the other camp and Jews from here were taken out. We didn't know what, what's their intention. But we found out after liberation they wanted to kill this group. Russians and Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or the Russian, Russian prisoners of war and the Jewish people that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also Russian prisoners of war partly. But you didn't know. They were all in prisoner uniform from that other camp which housed 10,000 Europeans. And the Russians were about roughly 2,000, 1,500 Russians there. And 1,000 of us. It was 2,500 people marching forward at a time in rows. We were given-- you take a blanket, half a bread, and a piece of margarine. And we went by walk. The, the beginning of the row already arrived to the station and the other end was still coming out from the camp. Karlsfeld. Would have been a column maybe five kilometers. [INAUDIBLE] lose in between them. No, no, Nobody walked fast anymore. There were no-- they didn't have enough power to, to walk too fast. And when we came to Karlsfeld there were wagons there. Coal wagons which was only a meter 20 high with wire on top. And the wagons, the carriages were filled with-- it's not [INAUDIBLE]. What they put in glass where not to break glass. It's like wood shaving fine. It's full of that. And we, we were still free. Didn't, didn't lock the wagons. And we were stationary in the train. They were still coming. And the river was next to Karlsfeld Station, was a little river. Fast moving river with a bank quite deep down, about four meter below the level. So you had a, a large bank. We got an alarm and, and some fighter planes came. Or fighter bombers, whatever they had that time. And they dropped a few bombs on the station. We, we were-- I was with my friends on, on the bank, sitting, laying on the bank and watching the station more or less protected from the river bank. And when that was over, we-- I had some Maggi soup cubes from-- German ones-- from the canteen. And they-- someone had a match. I didn't have matches on me. And we took this-- that [INAUDIBLE] or whatever from, from inside and warmed the soup. Took water from the river and in, in the two-liter jug which I carried, put the Maggi in and boiled the soup. Put a brick next to the track. Piece of rock, a brick. Put the fire underneath and boiled it up and made the soup. It was good and warm. And we were there about-- next, next the night came and early morning. Then they, they got the locomotive and started everybody in the wagon. They locked us in. You could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, again cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coal, the coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coal car, meter forty. You had to sit on that straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: The coal trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And woven thing-- you wouldn't even put your head out through there. It was woven. And I was approximately over the middle of, of the-- maybe was 20 carriages. And they whistled. They closed up and moved off. And we got to another station not too far. Was only, could have been 7:00 or-- between 7:00 and 9:00 in the morning when the locomotive got hit by one of these low flying aircraft. And we only knew some were injured in the first two carriages behind. The locomotive-- they pierced the thing, steam whistling, lost all the power. So we were stuck there maybe six hours in that track. And they brought another locomotive, electric. And we, we continued. We, we came to Staltach, the open place near Staltach. I didn't know where we were. Just the power stopped on the open tracks. There was no more power. There was no bombing. They must have bombed the place where the power is coming from. Could have been 50 kilometers away. Or transformers. And we stuck there. It was-- we were all wet. And it was rainy drizzle. Blankets got wet. And no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So parallel with, with the railway tracks, one, two tracks were there. One each way, the Germans had. But not a station, just an open track so actually no other train gets through. And we were sitting there hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And parallel about five, five meter away was a roadway. So you could see about two kilometers with, with the train station. And the other side of the roadway had bush and bits of field. And the Germans opened, opened the doors and let us off. No toilets, go in the bush. You, you were spreading out and guarding. And we found there potatoes buried in that lot. But they were cut or small potatoes. They don't bury the big ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- say, well, what about the other Germans? What about cooking some potatoes? They don't mind. They didn't care. We, we dug out some from the ground which was put in, sown in for next crop. And we were baking them there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then on the roadway a Swiss Red Cross car came past. And they stopped. They come to speak to the commander. This, this guards were different, not our guards. All SS and SS women. Because we had three-- lots of women there, Russian women. They're quite strong. They were working on railway construction, all with muscles. And they were guarded with these Russian SS-- with the German SS women. So the Red Cross car disappeared. And about two hours later, a five-ton truck came, also Red Cross marking. And we were given a three-kilo parcel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was American made. And the Germans were given one too, each, every guard, because they wouldn't agree to the whole thing if they would be cut out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fellow started eating. They had peanut butter and ordinary butter. They had a packet of cigarettes. Hard chocolate in a metal box. Dry rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they'd never eaten fatty stuff. They had dysentery from stuff they eat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some didn't eat. Some kept away from the butter. I know that they ate the biscuits. No chocolates. They knew that it was no good for them. They were so weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there till next, next day. And the Germans allowed to take the wires off. Next morning the sun start rising. You could see the vapors drying the blankets and the clothes and everything. And they let us off to go to the toilet, do our business, and that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't know till they were liberated in-- on, on that track that they had two big wagons behind the locomotive where there-- they were there to there two wagons full of food. Bread packed in with cheese and dry stuff. They never gave us. After liberation we went-- guys went in and some went on the roof and they threw the window out. Lots of food. Never given it. Was-- I don't know whether that food was for them or for us. Because if, if what, what we found out that the transport should, should have ended in, in southern Bavaria, in, in southern Tyrol, a place called-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] not [INAUDIBLE]-- Mittenwald. M-I-T-T-E-N-W-A-L-D, Mittenwald. And in Mittenwald, they had a ravine which was prepared to massacre prisoners by machine guns. Setup with different angles and everything else. They only needed a handful of, of submachine guns. You could-- they could kill thousands in, in a matter of hours. But that's how it happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, we, we got next day, about 11 o'clock, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Americans arrived. B The first one-- we saw tanks. We thought they are Russians, because they had the five stars, white stars with broken circle silk screened onto them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we never knew the Americans have a five star like the Russians. The Russian stuff is red, they have it white. So they came closer and closer. Then everybody come out of the wagons and go in front of them in the roadway. They-- some spoke Yiddish, some officers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some could speak a bit-- whatever language they could speak, they spoke to, to the people. Then we saw the Russian women there, talking to the guards. They're pulling their hair out, handfuls. SS guards, the women.

speaker: interviewee
text: The women guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when these Americans were the first tank unit, they-- each time the, the Germans put up some submachine in the bush, they were stopping and hitting that bush until 10, 15 minutes. There were no, no leaves left on, on, on the trees there. And then they kept going. They didn't, didn't take much risk, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how long-- how long were you traveling on the road from Allach before the-- before the Americans liberated you? How many-- how long did you travel from Allach on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we left the camp from Allach--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been about five days. We weren't traveling. We were sitting a day in Karlsfeld BMW. Then no electricity. And, and, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back onto the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Back onto the train, and back off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, back. And when we were liberated, the Americans-- one American officer said, just sit quiet because behind us is the Red Cross, and the infantry unit, and the supplies. And they came about-- roughly about an hour after this tank column. What they saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had the good, good shoes in the back of the tank. Just strings holding it, dangling in the-- in the air there. Rubber boots, all sorts of boots they can use it in different terrain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They saw this, wooden shoes. They gave it to us. They undone these shoes, whoever wanted it. Some fitted, some didn't. And rifles from German guard-- we had rifles. They took it from the guards. What we had rifles, the old, old rifles they had. Not the machine guns. The five-bullet rifles. If we had it, prisoners, they didn't touch it. When they found, they broke the wood part on the train's axle and they throw it on the heap. Then they took the guards in and took them prisoners. But these, these these two guys who live in Israel, three of us, we started walking, because we saw the station about-- in-- on the horizon still, station building. We come to the station, it says Staltach. We were at Staltach, and there's no houses here. So the houses were further in. We went into the village. We saw the first house. A note in German language, there's 10 prisoners living here. The next house, eight prisoners. And they were Yugoslav prisoners of war, not in pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go off with the-- with the Americans at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't ask you to come with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those who were sick were carried off, because the infantry came-- or they were not infantry. They were also in trucks, only not tank units. First aid and Red Cross and doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went down to probably-- wasn't too bad. Roughly between 35 and 40 kilo. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, we went to house to house to see where we can-- we enter a house which has no prisoners. So we found one place not only for prisoners. [INAUDIBLE] Let's go in. We're going in in the pajamas.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German people, elderly people, they were scared. The prisoners escaped, and they're going to kill them or whatever. They, they were patting us on the back. Come in the lunch room. And they had cakes and coffee. And these four Yugoslavs were there with their feet up on the table [LAUGHS] having a coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- afterwards, there was real coffee they had, from bean coffee. So we never had coffee, good coffee. So [INAUDIBLE] Silberstein just said to me, I think this, this coffee must have been poisoned. It tasted funny. [LAUGHS] He said-- it wasn't poisoned, just he didn't have real coffee. Why would they poison you in the last day? Of course it wasn't poisoned, because the Germans drink it, too. So we, we left the house. And we went into the village. We said-- we saw American military kitchens set up under canvas, no sides, only roof. And they were ready for dinner. And they were cooking and calling us in, giving us cheese and whatever they had got. Fried bread. And I said to myself, now what, what we do? Said no, [INAUDIBLE] we're going to sleep in a barn. The barn of a German house was built under the same roof. The house and the barn, the animals is under the same roof, not separated. It's a two-story place.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the haystack room, you can go in into the place where the bedrooms are. There's a door. So I told them, let's go. We have a bit of food here given by the Americans. We are really not hungry. Let's-- we put in our stuff in the hay. We took the square, a cube hay-- there were cubes there-- put it down and go in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still wearing your striped clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wearing the [INAUDIBLE] and the wooden shoes. And was lots of Americans in the village. You could hear the, the artillery in the-- in the far away at night, the noise of it still. So we weren't sure whether the Germans came back or not. We're a bit scared. And we found a, a store with shoes, shoe store. Not a shoe shop. A huge building. We went in and took proper shoes. We, we took not only one pair, as many as we could get. There were shoes from different countries of, of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: New shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: New shoes, all new in boxes. We took them out of the boxes and put them in our bag, jute bag. We took a whole bag, each of us. We put them in the-- in the stack. And we ate dinner and we went to sleep in there. And next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah. Thank you. We'll go on [? from there. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK that's the end of tape five of the interview with John Ullman. It's the 23rd of July, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
